title: Today's AI Brief: Jeff Bezos Is Funding a Wild Hunt for the Brain’s ‘Core Algorithm’
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including Jeff Bezos' funding for a search for the brain's 'core algorithm' and China's brain implant ambitions.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Jeff Bezos Is Funding a Wild Hunt for the Brain’s ‘Core Algorithm’
imageCaption: Jeff Bezos Is Funding a Wild Hunt for the Brain’s ‘Core Algorithm’
imageSourceTitle: Jeff Bezos Is Funding a Wild Hunt for the Brain’s ‘Core Algorithm’

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. We're diving into some fascinating developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Jeff Bezos is backing a quest to uncover the brain's 'core algorithm'. This is a pretty ambitious project - imagine trying to crack the code of human thought itself. I mean, it's not like they're trying to solve world hunger or anything, but still, it's a pretty cool idea.

According to Wired, Rob Williams pitched Jeff Bezos on a press release for a product that doesn't even exist yet - a chip that can supposedly read brain signals. Bezos gave it a thumbs up, and now the hunt is on for the brain's core algorithm. The idea is to develop a brain-computer interface that can decode neural activity and potentially treat a range of medical conditions. I'm curious to see how this one plays out - it's like something out of a sci-fi movie.

Next, I came across an article in MIT Technology Review about China's plans for brain implants. They've approved the world's first invasive brain-computer chip, which is a pretty significant step. This technology has the potential to help people with paralysis or other motor disorders, but it also raises questions about data boundaries and safety. I wonder what the regulatory framework will look like for this kind of tech.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
